# Bayesian Regression Models for Categorical Outcomes

Our last post covered how to do Bayesian regression for binary and count data in R using STAN. In this post, we’ll take a look at two useful regression models for categorical outcomes, namely ordinal regression for ordered categories and categorical/multinomial regression for unordered categories.

As mentioned before, in Bayesian modeling we define a statistical model, assign prior distributions to the unknown parameters, and use sampling methods such as Markov Chain Monte Carlo (MCMC) to obtain probabilistic estimates for the parameters.

## Ordinal Regression

Sometimes the outcome variable is categorical, but the categories have a natural order. A common example is a Likert scale such as strongly disagree, disagree, neutral, agree, and strongly agree. In this setting, standard logistic regression is not appropriate because it treats the outcome as binary, and multinomial regression is not ideal because it ignores the ordering information.

A common model for ordered outcomes is the cumulative logit model, also called ordinal logistic regression. The basic idea is that we model the cumulative probability of being at or below a category using a set of cutpoints together with a linear predictor. The model assumes that the effect of the predictors is the same across category thresholds, which is often called the proportional odds assumption.

The following code simulates ordinal data with one predictor.

```r
library(tidyverse)
library(kableExtra)
library(rstan)

set.seed(1234)

N <- 5000
x1 <- rnorm(N)

eta <- 1.2 * x1
cutpoints <- c(-1.5, -0.2, 1.0, 2.0)

z <- eta + rlogis(N)

y <- case_when(
  z <= cutpoints[1] ~ 1,
  z <= cutpoints[2] ~ 2,
  z <= cutpoints[3] ~ 3,
  z <= cutpoints[4] ~ 4,
  TRUE ~ 5
)

df_ord <- data.frame(y = y, x1 = x1)

head(df_ord) %>% kableExtra::kable()
```

Here the outcome takes values from 1 to 5, representing an ordered response scale. We generated the data using a latent-variable formulation, where the observed category depends on where the latent score falls relative to the cutpoints.

Now we fit the cumulative logit model in STAN.

```r
model_ord <- "
data {
  int<lower=1> N;
  int<lower=2> K_cat;
  vector[N] x;
  array[N] int<lower=1,upper=K_cat> y;
}
parameters {
  real beta;
  ordered[K_cat - 1] c;
}
model {
  beta ~ normal(0, 2.5);
  c ~ normal(0, 5);

  y ~ ordered_logistic(beta * x, c);
}
generated quantities {
  array[N] int y_rep;
  for (n in 1:N) {
    y_rep[n] = ordered_logistic_rng(beta * x[n], c);
  }
}
"
writeLines(model_ord, con = "model_ord.stan")
cat(model_ord)
```

```r
stan_data_ord <- list(
  N = nrow(df_ord),
  K_cat = 5,
  x = df_ord$x1,
  y = df_ord$y
)

fit_ord <- rstan::stan(
  file = "model_ord.stan",
  data = stan_data_ord,
  chains = 4,
  iter = 2000,
  warmup = 1000,
  seed = 1234
)
```

```r
fit_ord
```

The coefficient describes how the predictor shifts the latent tendency toward higher or lower categories. The cutpoints divide that latent scale into the observed response levels. A positive coefficient means that larger values of the predictor are associated with greater probability of being in higher outcome categories.

This model can also be extended to several predictors.

```r
set.seed(1234)

x1 <- rnorm(N)
x2 <- rnorm(N)
x3 <- rnorm(N)

eta <- 1 + 1.5 * x1 - 0.8 * x2 + 0.6 * x3
cutpoints <- c(-2, -0.5, 0.8, 2.2)

z <- eta + rlogis(N)

y <- case_when(
  z <= cutpoints[1] ~ 1,
  z <= cutpoints[2] ~ 2,
  z <= cutpoints[3] ~ 3,
  z <= cutpoints[4] ~ 4,
  TRUE ~ 5
)

df_ord2 <- data.frame(y = y, x1 = x1, x2 = x2, x3 = x3)

head(df_ord2) %>% kableExtra::kable()
```

```r
model_ord2 <- "
data {
  int<lower=1> N;
  int<lower=1> K;
  int<lower=2> K_cat;
  matrix[N, K] X;
  array[N] int<lower=1,upper=K_cat> y;
}
parameters {
  vector[K] beta;
  ordered[K_cat - 1] c;
}
model {
  beta ~ normal(0, 2.5);
  c ~ normal(0, 5);

  y ~ ordered_logistic(X * beta, c);
}
generated quantities {
  array[N] int y_rep;
  for (n in 1:N) {
    y_rep[n] = ordered_logistic_rng((X[n] * beta), c);
  }
}
"
writeLines(model_ord2, con = "model_ord2.stan")
cat(model_ord2)
```

```r
X_ord <- as.matrix(df_ord2[, c("x1", "x2", "x3")])

stan_data_ord2 <- list(
  N = nrow(df_ord2),
  K = ncol(X_ord),
  K_cat = 5,
  X = X_ord,
  y = df_ord2$y
)

fit_ord2 <- rstan::stan(
  file = "model_ord2.stan",
  data = stan_data_ord2,
  chains = 4,
  iter = 2000,
  warmup = 1000,
  seed = 1234
)
```

```r
fit_ord2
```

As with the logistic case, the posterior summaries should recover the simulated parameter values reasonably well. It is also helpful to inspect posterior predictive summaries to see whether the model reproduces the observed frequencies across the ordered categories.

## Categorical / Multinomial Regression

In other settings, the outcome variable has more than two categories but there is no natural ordering among them. Examples include preferred travel mode, brand choice, or diagnosis type. In this case, ordinal regression is not suitable because the categories are unordered, so we instead use categorical or multinomial regression.

This model extends logistic regression by modeling the probabilities of several classes at once. One category is usually treated as the reference, and the model estimates the log-odds of each remaining category relative to that baseline.

Let’s simulate data for a multinomial regression model with three outcome categories.

```r
set.seed(1234)

N <- 5000
x1 <- rnorm(N)

eta1 <- 0.5 + 1.0 * x1
eta2 <- -0.5 - 1.2 * x1

exp_eta1 <- exp(eta1)
exp_eta2 <- exp(eta2)

denom <- 1 + exp_eta1 + exp_eta2

p1 <- 1 / denom
p2 <- exp_eta1 / denom
p3 <- exp_eta2 / denom

y <- map_int(1:N, function(i) {
  sample(1:3, size = 1, prob = c(p1[i], p2[i], p3[i]))
})

df_cat <- data.frame(y = y, x1 = x1)

head(df_cat) %>% kableExtra::kable()
```

Here we have three classes, and the probabilities are determined by a softmax-style transformation so that they are all positive and sum to one.

Now we fit a categorical regression model in STAN.

```r
model_cat <- "
data {
  int<lower=1> N;
  int<lower=2> K_cat;
  vector[N] x;
  array[N] int<lower=1,upper=K_cat> y;
}
parameters {
  vector[K_cat - 1] alpha;
  vector[K_cat - 1] beta;
}
model {
  alpha ~ normal(0, 5);
  beta ~ normal(0, 2.5);

  for (n in 1:N) {
    vector[K_cat] eta;
    eta[1] = 0;
    for (k in 2:K_cat) {
      eta[k] = alpha[k - 1] + beta[k - 1] * x[n];
    }
    y[n] ~ categorical_logit(eta);
  }
}
generated quantities {
  array[N] int y_rep;
  for (n in 1:N) {
    vector[K_cat] eta;
    eta[1] = 0;
    for (k in 2:K_cat) {
      eta[k] = alpha[k - 1] + beta[k - 1] * x[n];
    }
    y_rep[n] = categorical_logit_rng(eta);
  }
}
"
writeLines(model_cat, con = "model_cat.stan")
cat(model_cat)
```

```r
stan_data_cat <- list(
  N = nrow(df_cat),
  K_cat = 3,
  x = df_cat$x1,
  y = df_cat$y
)

fit_cat <- rstan::stan(
  file = "model_cat.stan",
  data = stan_data_cat,
  chains = 4,
  iter = 2000,
  warmup = 1000,
  seed = 1234
)
```

```r
fit_cat
```

The intercepts and slopes are interpreted relative to the baseline class. For example, a positive coefficient for category 2 means that increasing the predictor raises the log-odds of category 2 relative to category 1.

As before, this can be extended to several predictors.

```r
set.seed(1234)

x1 <- rnorm(N)
x2 <- rnorm(N)
x3 <- rnorm(N)

eta1 <- 0.5 + 1.2 * x1 - 0.7 * x2 + 0.3 * x3
eta2 <- -0.8 - 1.0 * x1 + 0.9 * x2 - 0.4 * x3

exp_eta1 <- exp(eta1)
exp_eta2 <- exp(eta2)

denom <- 1 + exp_eta1 + exp_eta2

p1 <- 1 / denom
p2 <- exp_eta1 / denom
p3 <- exp_eta2 / denom

y <- map_int(1:N, function(i) {
  sample(1:3, size = 1, prob = c(p1[i], p2[i], p3[i]))
})

df_cat2 <- data.frame(y = y, x1 = x1, x2 = x2, x3 = x3)

head(df_cat2) %>% kableExtra::kable()
```

```r
model_cat2 <- "
data {
  int<lower=1> N;
  int<lower=1> K;
  int<lower=2> K_cat;
  matrix[N, K] X;
  array[N] int<lower=1,upper=K_cat> y;
}
parameters {
  matrix[K_cat - 1, K] beta;
  vector[K_cat - 1] alpha;
}
model {
  to_vector(beta) ~ normal(0, 2.5);
  alpha ~ normal(0, 5);

  for (n in 1:N) {
    vector[K_cat] eta;
    eta[1] = 0;
    for (k in 2:K_cat) {
      eta[k] = alpha[k - 1] + X[n] * beta[k - 1]';
    }
    y[n] ~ categorical_logit(eta);
  }
}
generated quantities {
  array[N] int y_rep;
  for (n in 1:N) {
    vector[K_cat] eta;
    eta[1] = 0;
    for (k in 2:K_cat) {
      eta[k] = alpha[k - 1] + X[n] * beta[k - 1]';
    }
    y_rep[n] = categorical_logit_rng(eta);
  }
}
"
writeLines(model_cat2, con = "model_cat2.stan")
cat(model_cat2)
```

```r
X_cat <- as.matrix(df_cat2[, c("x1", "x2", "x3")])

stan_data_cat2 <- list(
  N = nrow(df_cat2),
  K = ncol(X_cat),
  K_cat = 3,
  X = X_cat,
  y = df_cat2$y
)

fit_cat2 <- rstan::stan(
  file = "model_cat2.stan",
  data = stan_data_cat2,
  chains = 4,
  iter = 2000,
  warmup = 1000,
  seed = 1234
)
```

```r
fit_cat2
```

Again, the posterior means should be close to the values used to simulate the data. In practice, predicted category probabilities are often easier to interpret than raw coefficients, especially when there are several outcome classes.

## Model Checking

As in our previous examples, fitting the model is only part of the workflow. It is useful to check convergence diagnostics such as `Rhat` and effective sample size, and to use posterior predictive checks to compare observed category frequencies to those generated by the fitted model.

For ordinal models, it is also worth thinking carefully about the proportional odds assumption. If that assumption is not reasonable, then a more flexible model may be needed. For categorical regression, it is often helpful to plot predicted class probabilities as a function of the predictors rather than focusing only on coefficient tables.

## Conclusion

This tutorial provided a quick overview of how to fit ordinal and categorical regression models in STAN using the `rstan` interface. Ordinal regression is useful when the outcome categories have a natural order, while categorical or multinomial regression is appropriate when the outcome classes are unordered.

These models extend the ideas of logistic regression to richer outcome types and are useful in many applied settings. Future posts can build on this by looking at hierarchical versions of these models, partial pooling across groups, and more detailed posterior predictive checks.
